Title: Lonicera caerulea genome reveals molecular mechanisms of freezing tolerance and anthocyanin biosynthesis

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: UDP-glycosyltransferase family; PSPG motif; UGT78 family
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Extremely highly expressed at mature fruit stages Fr6-Fr8 (FPKM >1000)
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High expression in mature fruits during Fr6-Fr8; increased with fruit development
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Fruit pigmentation and antioxidant/stress-related protective roles
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Efficiently promotes accumulation of cyanidin-3-O-glucoside as the major anthocyanin in L. caerulea fruits
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in E. coli; purified recombinant protein in vitro enzyme assay; LC-MS/MS product identification; enzyme kinetics; expression profiling during fruit development; comparative assay with orthologs
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LcUGT78X1 catalyzes 3-O-glucosylation of cyanidin, pelargonidin, and delphinidin. 2. It shows regiospecific production of monoglycosides and high catalytic efficiency toward cyanidin. 3. Its fruit-maturation expression pattern matches cyanidin-3-O-glucoside accumulation, supporting a major role in its biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit developmental stage; anthocyanin-related cold response context discussed in paper
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for metabolic engineering or breeding to enhance cyanidin-3-O-glucoside accumulation in fruit due to high efficiency and lack of substrate inhibition
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although enzymatically a glycosyltransferase, the paper particularly emphasizes its decisive contribution to cyanidin-3-O-glucoside accumulation and product specificity; categorized here as Common Gene because requested scheme defines non-regulatory, non-classical pathway/storage-related genes separately when appropriate.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in mature fruits; also upregulated under cold stress
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression correlates with fruit mature stages; increased under cold treatment
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Fruit pigmentation and antioxidant/stress-related protective roles
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to anthocyanin glycosylation, including formation of cyanidin-3-O-glycoside, but with broader product profile than LcUGT78X1
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in E. coli; purified recombinant protein in vitro enzyme assay; LC-MS/MS product identification; expression profiling
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LcUGT95P1 glycosylates cyanidin, pelargonidin, and delphinidin at 3-OH. 2. It also generates additional mono- and diglycosylated products from several substrates. 3. The gene is associated with fruit anthocyanin accumulation and is induced by cold stress.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broader product promiscuity may reduce product specificity relative to LcUGT78X1
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering diversified anthocyanin glycosides, though less product-specific than LcUGT78X1
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalyzes multiple mono- and diglycosides, indicating lower regiospecificity than LcUGT78X1.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Peonidin from cyanidin; petunidin and malvidin from delphinidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers methyl group(s) from SAM to anthocyanidins, methylating cyanidin, delphinidin, and petunidin at 3' and/or 5' hydroxyl positions
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms modified anthocyanidins derived from the target anthocyanin branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: methylated anthocyanidins including peonidin, petunidin, and malvidin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanidin methylation and expands anthocyanin structural diversity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli; purified protein in vitro enzyme assay; HPLC/LC-MS product identification; expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed at late fruit stages Fr6-Fr8
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High expression during late fruit development, consistent with anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate enzyme for engineering methylated anthocyanin diversity in berry crops
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LcOMT2 is highly expressed during late fruit maturation. 2. It methylates cyanidin to peonidin and delphinidin toward petunidin/malvidin. 3. It displays substrate promiscuity and contributes to anthocyanin diversification.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No activity detected toward pelargonidin or peonidin in assays.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Peonidin from cyanidin; petunidin and malvidin from delphinidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers methyl group(s) from SAM to anthocyanidins, with weaker activity on cyanidin and activity on delphinidin methylation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms modified anthocyanidins derived from the target anthocyanin branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: methylated anthocyanidins including peonidin, petunidin, and malvidin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to anthocyanidin methylation with weaker cyanidin activity than LcOMT2/LcOMT20
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli; purified protein in vitro enzyme assay; HPLC/LC-MS product identification; expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed at late fruit stages Fr6-Fr8
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High expression during late fruit development
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Weaker activity toward cyanidin may limit utility for peonidin enrichment
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential component for enzyme stacking to diversify methylated anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LcOMT14 is a fruit-expressed anthocyanin OMT. 2. It methylates cyanidin only slightly and also methylates delphinidin sequentially. 3. It supports formation of methylated anthocyanidin derivatives in L. caerulea.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No activity toward pelargonidin or peonidin was detected in assays.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Peonidin from cyanidin; petunidin and malvidin from delphinidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers methyl group(s) from SAM to anthocyanidins, efficiently methylating cyanidin and delphinidin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms modified anthocyanidins derived from the target anthocyanin branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: methylated anthocyanidins including peonidin, petunidin, and malvidin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanidin methylation and broadens anthocyanin diversity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cloning; heterologous expression in E. coli; purified protein in vitro enzyme assay; HPLC/LC-MS product identification; expression profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed at late fruit stages Fr6-Fr8
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High expression during late fruit development, consistent with anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising enzyme for engineering methylated anthocyanin composition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LcOMT20 is strongly expressed in late fruit development. 2. It efficiently methylates cyanidin and delphinidin-derived substrates. 3. It functions with LcOMT2 and LcOMT14 as an anthocyanin 3',5'-O-methyltransferase set in L. caerulea.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No activity toward pelargonidin or peonidin was detected in assays.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Freezing tolerance-associated stress acclimation linked to protective metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: COR genes including LcCOR1; LcCOR3; LcCOR5
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates cold-regulated genes in ICE-CBF-COR pathway
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Freezing/cold stress; ICE1 and CAMTA inputs
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated after 2 h freezing treatment at -20, -40, and -80 °C
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Rapidly induced in dormant buds under freezing conditions
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with enhanced freezing tolerance through activation of cold-response network
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative transcriptomics; DEG analysis; co-expression network analysis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Multiple LcCBFs are rapidly induced by freezing stress. 2. They act as hub genes in the cold-response co-expression network. 3. They likely activate COR genes and are central regulators of freezing tolerance.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Freezing treatments at 4, -20, -40, and -80 °C for different durations
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential targets to improve freezing tolerance, though direct metabolic quality effects were not functionally engineered here
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These are core freezing-tolerance regulators rather than direct anthocyanin biosynthetic enzymes.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Upstream transcriptional activation in cold signaling
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated after 2 h freezing treatment at -20, -40, and -80 °C
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in dormant buds by freezing
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positively associated with freezing tolerance via activation of downstream CBF-COR signaling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Several LcICE1 genes are induced by freezing. 2. They are positioned upstream of CBFs in the proposed cold-acclimation model. 3. They contribute to the freezing tolerance regulatory network.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Supported mainly by expression/network inference, not transgenic validation in this paper.

Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: ABA signaling via LcPYL4/LcPYL5-LcPP2C6-LcSNRK2-3 pathway; freezing stress
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated after freezing treatment with similar trend to ABA signaling genes
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced in dormant buds under freezing stress
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with promotion of freezing tolerance through ABA signaling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LcABF5 is induced by freezing stress. 2. Its expression parallels ABA pathway activation. 3. It supports the conclusion that ABA signaling contributes to freezing tolerance in L. caerulea.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for improving cold tolerance via ABA-responsive pathways
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct downstream target genes were experimentally validated in this study.
